title: The Center
subtitle: Staking your claim
summary: Meet the four central squares and learn why grabbing them is the first job of the opening.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System (public domain).

steps.title: Meet the center
steps.markdown: The four squares in the middle — **e4, d4, e5, and d5** — are the [[center]]. Pieces standing here reach the most squares, so the player who controls the center usually controls the game. Your very first job in a game is to fight for it.

steps.title: Grab a center square
steps.markdown: Push one of your center pawns two squares forward to claim space. Either the king's pawn or the queen's pawn does the job.

hints.text: A pawn move is best — knights and bishops come next.
Try the pawn in front of your king.

feedback.correct: Yes! You've planted a pawn in the center and opened lines for your pieces.
feedback.wrong: That move doesn't fight for the center yet. Push a center pawn forward.

steps.title: Build a healthy opening
steps.markdown: Now play out a classic opening. Take the center, then [[development|develop]] a knight to attack, then a bishop, gaining [[tempo]]. The computer answers for Black.

hints.text: Knights before bishops. Aim your pieces at the center.
Your knight belongs on f3, attacking e5.

feedback.correct: Great — every move develops a piece toward the center.
feedback.wrong: Develop a piece toward the center (a knight or bishop), don't push a random pawn.

steps.title: Read the position
steps.markdown: You've reached a famous position (the Ruy Lopez). Your bishop just pinned... no — it eyes the knight on c6. What is your main idea here?

options.label: Pressure the knight that guards e5
options.explain: Right! Bxc6 followed by Nxe5 is the point — you're chipping at Black's center pawn.

options.label: Deliver checkmate right now
options.explain: Not yet — there's no mate in the position. Good opening play is about small advantages.

options.label: Castle the bishop to safety
options.explain: Bishops can't castle — only the king does, with a rook. You'll castle your king soon, though!

steps.title: What you learned
steps.markdown: Three ideas to keep forever: **fight for the center**, **develop a piece every move**, and **knights before bishops**. Do that and you'll have a healthy position out of every opening.
